query: Inferior thyroid artery ligation during thyroidectomy

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroidectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Blunt dissection then progresses through the loose areolar tissue covering the thyroid gland, extending laterally until the carotid sheath is reached, which defines the lateral boundary of the dissection. This step can be performed with careful finger dissection or with bipolar cautery retraction. The middle thyroid vein may appear at this stage and should be ligated. In cases where a large goiter obstructs access, division of the strap muscles may be required to improve exposure. If division is necessary, it should be performed as superiorly as possible to maintain innervation by the ansa cervicalis and preserve muscle function.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroidectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Inferior pole dissection: During the dissection of the thyroid’s inferior pole, the branches of the inferior thyroid artery and vein must be ligated individually due to their typical branching pattern before entering the gland. Identifying the recurrent laryngeal nerve before addressing the inferior pole vessels is strongly recommended to improve surgical safety, as this nerve often lies near these vessels. This careful approach minimizes the risk of accidental nerve injury during vessel ligation, allowing for safer dissection and protecting the patient’s vocal function.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Arteries -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: During a parathyroidectomy or thyroidectomy (total or partial), careful attention should be given to the arteries of the thyroid as well as coursing nerves. The recurrent laryngeal nerve, a branch of CN X courses with the inferior thyroid artery, while dissecting the ITA insult to the recurrent laryngeal nerve will cause the patient to go hoarse as this nerve innervates the larynx. Often during thyroidectomies, surgeons leave one parathyroid gland as not to cause the patient to become hypocalcemic. [8] As the inferior thyroid arteries mainly supply the parathyroid glands, accidental ligation can result in hypoparathyroidism.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroidectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Superior pole dissection: The thyroid lobe is gently retracted anteromedially to expose the superior thyroid artery and vein. The avascular plane between the superior pole vessels laterally and the cricothyroid muscle medially is carefully developed using blunt dissection, working from an inferior to superior direction. This process creates a dissection window just below the superior thyroid vessels. Keeping dissection close to the thyroid capsule helps reduce the risk of damaging the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve. The superior thyroid vessels are then secured with ligatures, surgical clips, or energy devices, and the surrounding loose areolar tissue is divided once these vessels are ligated. Precision during this step is essential to avoid injuring the superior parathyroid glands. By ligating the superior pole vessels, the thyroid lobe can be elevated and rotated medially, which aids in exposing the tracheoesophageal groove and the area of the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Technique or Treatment -- Patient Positioning and Exposure
retrieved_docs.content: A longitudinal or oblique incision along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid (SCM) muscle is the most commonly preferred approach. A transverse incision may offer better cosmetic results and is technically feasible except in patients with high carotid bifurcations. Electrocautery is used to divide the platysma and dissect along the medial border of the SCM, extending from its superior tendon to the omohyoid muscle inferiorly. The internal jugular vein (IJV) is exposed via sharp dissection. The common facial and middle thyroid veins are ligated inferiorly, and hypoglossal veins superiorly, facilitating lateral retraction of the SCM and IJV.

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Technique or Treatment -- Patient Positioning and Exposure
retrieved_docs.content: Meticulous identification and preservation of cranial nerves are paramount. The vagus nerve, usually posterolateral to the carotid artery, may lie anteriorly and is vulnerable during early dissection. The ansa cervicalis is divided near its origin and followed cephalad to the hypoglossal nerve. The superior thyroid artery is dissected only to its origin to avoid injury to the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve. Distal ICA exposure may require dividing the digastric muscle and ligating overlying veins for high lesions or long plaques. Cephalad mobilization of the hypoglossal nerve may involve division of the occipital or sternocleidomastoid arteries. Adjunctive techniques, such as mandibular osteotomy, cutting the occipital artery, or sectioning the ansa cervicalis, may improve exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroidectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Raising subplatysmal flaps: Subplatysmal flaps are carefully elevated superiorly and inferiorly, with the platysma retracted superiorly to enhance exposure. A combination of blunt and sharp dissection, aided by electrocautery, allows for the extension of the subplatysmal plane from the thyroid cartilage to the sternal notch. Achieving an adequate plane here is critical, especially when using a minimally invasive incision, as it directly impacts surgical exposure. Preserving the anterior jugular veins is essential; these veins should stay posterior to the dissection plane to avoid unintended injury. Ligation can effectively control bleeding if the anterior jugular vein is inadvertently damaged.

retrieved_docs.title: Parathyroidectomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Ligate the middle thyroid vein, then retract the thyroid lobe medially and anteriorly; the recurrent laryngeal nerve and the parathyroid glands can be identified at the upper and lower poles of the thyroid lobe. A similar exploration is made on the other side.  The serum sample of PTH is collected for the baseline level.

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Technique or Treatment -- Dissection and Endarterectomy
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic heparinization is performed after vessel control with loops and clips (eg, 2-0 silk on the superior thyroid artery, and Schwartz clip for distal ICA). A microscope or exoscope is used for precision. A longitudinal arteriotomy is made, and a shunt is placed if indicated. The atheroma is elevated in a medial plane, with the proximal plaque sharply divided from the healthy CCA and extending cephalad to a feathered endpoint in the ICA. In patients with redundant ICA, the artery may be shortened and reimplanted more proximally. Eversion endarterectomy is used when appropriate and involves transecting the ICA and inverting it to allow blunt, circumferential medial dissection: Type I transection For plaque localized to the ICA Type II transection For plaque involving the distal CCA and proximal ICA, typically 3 mm below the flow divider

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Muscles -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The thyroid muscles and surrounding vasculature are essential to consider during surgery in the neck region. The infrahyoid muscles may require incision during thyroid surgery to increase exposure to the thyroid gland. There has been some evidence to show that injury to the infrahyoid muscles during thyroid surgery may impact patients’ voices. The primary finding regarding this injury is that patients’ ability to reach high pitches decreased postoperatively. Additionally, when dissecting the superior pole of the thyroid, it is important not to perform high ligation of the superior thyroid artery. The reason behind this is that the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve is in the vicinity and can be injured. Thus, the patient will present with dysphonia if the nerve has suffered an injury. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroidectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Recurrent laryngeal nerve identification: Identification of the nerve typically follows exposure of the superior pole vessels. The recurrent laryngeal nerve generally courses from an inferolateral to superomedial direction along the prevertebral fascia before penetrating the cricothyroid membrane to innervate the laryngeal muscles. Key anatomical landmarks assist in locating the nerve, including its usual position posterior to the tubercle of Zuckerkandl, anterior to the inferior parathyroid glands, and posterior to the superior parathyroid glands. The nerve often lies near the branches of the inferior thyroid artery, making early ligation of this artery a potential risk factor for nerve injury. [21] [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Technique or Treatment -- Radical Neck Dissection
retrieved_docs.content: Next, the tendons of the digastric and stylohyoid muscles are divided while protecting the hypoglossal nerve (CN XII), allowing for access to the superior aspect of the IJV and the submandibular region contents. The facial artery and vein are ligated on the inferior aspect of the submandibular gland. The submandibular gland and associated lymph nodes are then reflected superiorly and raised off of the mylohyoid. The mylohyoid is retracted anteriorly to reveal the course of CN XII along with the lingual nerve and submandibular duct. The duct and submandibular ganglion are then ligated, preserving the lingual nerve. The facial vessels will again be encountered on the anterior aspect of the submandibular gland and require a second ligation. The submandibular gland is then dissected free from the mandible and reflected posteriorly, exposing the superior aspect of the IJV. The IJV is then ligated high in the neck.

retrieved_docs.title: Substernal Thyroidectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: During a total thyroidectomy, it is often useful to commence with mobilization of the smaller lobe to facilitate the excision of the whole gland and the identification of the recurrent laryngeal nerve and the parathyroid glands. Preservation of the parathyroid glands, especially the superior ones, is of great importance. In cases of very large goiters, the sternothyroid muscles can be divided unilaterally or bilaterally to help identify and ligate the middle thyroid veins, preserve the anatomical structures, and mobilize the goiter. Additionally, the isthmus can be divided and the two lobes removed separately. Mobilization of the upper thyroid lobes facilitates traction of the gland towards the head and excision of the retrosternal goiter.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid -- Surgical Considerations -- Superior Laryngeal Nerve
retrieved_docs.content: Dissection of the superior pole of the thyroid gland may expose the superior laryngeal nerve (SLN), which frequently courses adjacent to the superior thyroid artery. High ligation of the artery increases the risk of injuring the SLN, particularly its external branch. Approximately 20% of patients are vulnerable to SLN injury when the superior thyroid vessels are clamped, divided, and ligated en masse. Dividing the artery as it enters the thyroid capsule minimizes this risk. Most surgeons do not require direct visualization of the SLN during dissection.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroidectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Parathyroid gland identification: Identifying and preserving the parathyroid glands is essential in thyroidectomy to prevent hypoparathyroidism and maintain calcium homeostasis. While often described as a distinct step, parathyroid identification occurs progressively during the procedure. The superior parathyroid glands generally have a consistent location on the posterior aspect of the upper thyroid lobe, approximately 1 cm above the intersection of the recurrent laryngeal nerve and the inferior thyroid artery. The recurrent laryngeal nerve typically runs anterior to the superior parathyroid glands. Conversely, the inferior parathyroid glands display greater anatomical variability, often near the inferior thyroid artery and typically positioned anteriorly to the recurrent laryngeal nerve. [24] Care must be taken during dissection to preserve the delicate vascular supply of the parathyroid glands, which is crucial for maintaining their function. In cases where a parathyroid gland is inadvertently excised or becomes ischemic, autotransplantation can be performed by implanting the gland into the sternocleidomastoid or brachialis muscle belly, offering the potential for functional restoration.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroidectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Specimen removal: Once the inferior pole has been dissected, the thyroid lobe is primarily attached only at the tubercle of Zuckerkandl and the ligament of Berry. These structures are relatively avascular, allowing for separation using sharp dissection or an energy device. During this step, special care must be taken to avoid injury to the underlying trachea. In the case of a total thyroidectomy, the same procedure is then repeated on the opposite side. If intraoperative nerve monitoring is used, verifying the integrity of the recurrent laryngeal nerve and preserving the parathyroid glands is essential before continuing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the anatomic site associated with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer is classified under the broader category of thyroid disorders (اضطراب صماوي) and is clinically linked to the surgical removal of the thyroid gland. Surgical management of thyroid disease includes thyroidectomy, which may be performed as a complete removal (استئصال الدرقية) or as a partial removal (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي). Both complete and partial thyroidectomy are sub‑procedures encompassed by the general procedure termed “thyroidectomy; partial or complete.” The head and neck region is therefore the target anatomy for these thyroidectomy procedures. Additionally, partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with the anatomical reference “دراق,” reflecting its relationship to the thyroid gland itself. Understanding the connections among the head and neck anatomy, thyroid cancer, underlying thyroid disorders, and the spectrum of thyroidectomy techniques is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2171
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy, which may be performed as either a partial (lobectomy) or a complete (total) removal of thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 933
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of the thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically associated with the procedure استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). Thyroidectomy, whether performed as a partial or complete operation (thyroidectomy; partial or complete), is a targeted procedure for the head and neck structure and serves as the parent procedure for both استئصال الدرقية (full thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). The partial thyroidectomy (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي) frequently co‑occurs with دراق, reflecting a contextual relationship. Understanding these interconnections is essential for planning appropriate surgical management of thyroid cancer within the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.content: The term استئصال الدرقية is the Arabic name for thyroidectomy, a surgical procedure that involves removing all or part of the thyroid gland; it may be performed as a partial or a complete thyroidectomy depending on the underlying thyroid condition being treated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1146
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the thyroid is classified as a subtype of اضطراب صماوي (thyroid disorder) and is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). The broader procedure category thyroidectomy; partial or complete serves as a parent concept for both استئصال الدرقية (total thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). Partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with دراق (the thyroid gland) itself, reflecting the close anatomical and therapeutic relationship between the gland and its surgical removal. This network of relationships underscores that thyroid cancer is viewed within the spectrum of thyroid disorders and that its management commonly involves either total or partial thyroidectomy, each linked to the underlying thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as تروية دموية and is a subcategory of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck, indicating that interventions directed at blood vessels are considered vascularization procedures and belong to the broader group of head and neck vessel procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2203
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically linked to the surgical treatment known as thyroidectomy; partial or complete, a procedure that specifically targets the head and neck structure. The term thyroidectomy; partial or complete is a detailed form of the broader Arabic term استئصال الدرقية, which serves as its parent designation. This relationship underscores that thyroidectomy (partial or complete) is the primary operative intervention for managing cancer of the thyroid within the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1879
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of operative techniques and is positioned within the larger group of Clinical actions A‑G. It encompasses arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from joints, multiple forms of cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy, and cholangiography), and cranial procedures such as exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region and suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression. Excision also covers orbital exploration by a transcranial approach, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, foraminotomy, partial facetectomy, and disc excision, as well as nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision. Parathyroid surgery falls under this umbrella, including standard parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, and combined mediastinal‑exploration procedures. In addition to these methods, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and maintains a specific relationship with plastic procedures on the nose. Certain extensive spinal procedures—such as multisegment thoracic laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. Together, these relationships define Excision as a versatile operative concept that integrates numerous specialized techniques while delineating its scope relative to broader clinical actions and excluded methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1619
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is described as a broad surgical approach that encompasses a variety of operative techniques used to remove tissue, explore anatomical regions, or treat disease. Specific procedures classified under the Excision method include partial or complete adrenalectomy with or without adrenal gland biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal routes; cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, with or without choledochoenterostomy; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial locations; suboccipital craniectomy aimed at cranial‑nerve exploration or decompression; radical orchiectomy for tumor removal that incorporates abdominal exploration; and parathyroidectomy procedures that involve mediastinal or cervical exploration, sometimes requiring a sternal split or transthoracic approach. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576
retrieved_docs.content: Excision functions as an overarching surgical category that includes a wide variety of operative techniques. Specific procedures classified as methods of Excision are Adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy (performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally as a separate procedure); Cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct accompanied by cholangiography; Cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with or without cholangiography); Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region; Exploration of the orbit by a transcranial approach with lesion removal; Laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with decompression of cervical nerve roots, including partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and excision of herniated intervertebral disc; Laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration; Parathyroidectomy or exploration

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1831
retrieved_docs.content: Head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of thyroid, indicating that thyroid malignancies arise within this region. Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي (endocrine disorder), linking the disease to broader hormonal system pathology. The primary therapeutic intervention for thyroid cancer is thyroidectomy; partial or complete, a surgical procedure that is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (the Arabic term for thyroidectomy). This procedure is also recognized as the target anatomy–procedure relationship for the head and neck structure, reinforcing its role in the management of thyroid disease. The synonym relationship between thyroidectomy; partial or complete and استئصال الدرقية further clarifies that both terms refer to the same surgical treatment. Together, these connections underscore the anatomical, pathological, and therapeutic links among head and neck structures, thyroid cancer, endocrine disorders, and thyroidectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2173
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a subtype of اضطراب صماوي (endocrine disorder) and is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy), reflecting its categorization within endocrine pathologies and the common surgical intervention used in its treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
retrieved_docs.content: Excision serves as a broad parent category for a variety of surgical actions, including the set of procedures labeled “Clinical actions A‑G.” Within this overarching category, many specific operative methods are classified as forms of excision: cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (both open and laparoscopic), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) that decompress nerve roots and may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. At the same time, the excision category explicitly excludes several laminectomy techniques used for spinal stenosis: thoracic laminectomy with cord or cauda equina decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two vertebral segments; and cervical, lumbar, or thoracic laminectomy for more than two vertebral segments performed under the same constraints. Excision is also linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional techniques extend beyond deep organ surgery to include facial and upper aerodigestive tract interventions. This integrated view highlights excision as a versatile surgical concept that encompasses numerous specific operative methods while delineating clear boundaries for procedures it does not cover.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy; partial or complete, indicating that removal of the thyroid gland—whether in part or in entirety—is performed within this anatomical region. This procedure is also known by its Arabic name استئصال الدرقية, which serves as the parent term for the English description of thyroidectomy; partial or complete. Understanding that the thyroid gland resides in the head and neck area and recognizing both the English and Arabic terminology helps clinicians accurately plan and communicate surgical interventions for thyroid disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 761
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedure is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent grouping “Clinical actions A – G” and comprises a wide array of specific operative techniques. It includes adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct and cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage or removal of calculi, which may be accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region; exploration of the orbit by a transcranial approach with lesion removal; numerous laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord and/or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy or discectomy—ranging from 1‑2 vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis) and thoracic spine to more than two segments in the cervical and thoracic spine; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and/or excision of a herniated intervertebral disc in single cervical or lumbar interspaces; nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct; and parathyroidectomy or exploration of the parathyroids, including variants with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical, sternal‑split or transthoracic approaches. Some of these methods are listed as possibly included within the exploration category, reflecting flexibility in how they may be combined or selected. Together, these procedures illustrate the diverse yet interrelated nature of exploratory surgeries used for diagnostic assessment and therapeutic intervention across multiple organ systems.
